hindi: तिल सॉस के साथ चीनी पत्तागोभी

food_subgroup: Dressed vegetable salad / cold vegetable dish with sesame sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a Chinese cabbage food product class but no specific entry for sesame-dressed cold cabbage preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers Chinese cabbage as a raw vegetable. No specific code exists for the dressed/prepared dish. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific prepared Chinese cabbage dish.

main_ingredients: napa cabbage (Chinese cabbage / Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis)
sesame paste (tahini or Chinese sesame paste)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Chinese Cabbage with Sesame Sauce is approximately 150 g, presented as a cold side dish or starter.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese cabbage (pe-tsai), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11119; Sesame sauce / tahini — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #12166; composite estimation for dressed dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete dressed dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for raw napa cabbage (approximately 80% by weight) and sesame paste/tahini (approximately 15% by weight) with minor contributions from soy sauce, sesame oil, and vinegar. Energy and macros are calculated from these proportions. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of vegetables and mixed vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on napa cabbage and sesame-dressed vegetable salad data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Qianlong Bai Cai. GI estimated at 15 based on raw napa cabbage (GI ~10–20, low category per University of Sydney GI Database) and the low carbohydrate contribution of sesame sauce. The dish is predominantly non-starchy vegetable with fat and protein from sesame, which further suppresses glycemic response. GL per 150 g serving = (15 × 7.4 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.7, confirming very low glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce in the sesame dressing
hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving provides approximately 465 mg sodium, which represents about 20% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of only 1.7 per serving, this dish has a negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unsaturated fat profile from sesame and absence of cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The sesame paste contributes predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats with zero cholesterol, which supports a heart-healthy lipid profile; however, sodium from soy sauce should be monitored in patients with hypertensive cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Cabbage with Sesame Sauce, known in Chinese as 乾隆白菜 (Qiánlóng Báicài), is a classic cold vegetable dish from Chinese imperial court cuisine. According to culinary tradition, the dish was encountered by the Qing dynasty Emperor Qianlong during his southern tours of China, where he reportedly enjoyed its simple elegance. The dish consists of fresh napa cabbage (Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis) that is lightly blanched or served raw, then dressed with a rich sesame-based sauce made from sesame paste or tahini, sesame oil, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and garlic. The result is a refreshing, savoury, and slightly nutty cold salad with a crisp-tender texture and a complex umami-sesame flavour profile. In Singapore, it is served as a cold starter or side dish in Chinese restaurants. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (approximately 98 kcal per 100 g), provides moderate protein from sesame, and delivers predominantly unsaturated fats. It is rich in folate, vitamin C, and calcium from the cabbage and sesame. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate due to soy sauce and should be noted by those managing hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, though it contains sesame allergen and may contain gluten from soy sauce.

How many calories in sesame cabbage?

meal_context: part of a shared Chinese meal
suitable for vegetarian and vegan diners

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers the complete dressed dish. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy data for raw napa cabbage and sesame paste/tahini. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for non-starchy vegetables. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates: approximately 80% raw napa cabbage (USDA SR Legacy #11119) and approximately 15% sesame paste/tahini (USDA SR Legacy #12166), with minor contributions from soy sauce, sesame oil, and vinegar. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for all component ingredients). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients present; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for all component ingredients). 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for sesame paste and raw cabbage). 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — entirely plant-based dish; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). 6. GI set to 15 as an analogous estimate for this low-GI non-starchy vegetable dish dressed with sesame; gi_category low per University of Sydney GI Database convention for non-starchy vegetables. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are reasonable approximations. 8. Food group corrected from input 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Vegetables' as the primary ingredient is napa cabbage; sesame sauce is a condiment component but does not define the food group of the dish. 9. Allergen note: contains sesame; soy sauce may contain wheat/gluten — not gluten-free. 10. Sodium is moderate (~310 mg/100 g, ~465 mg per 150 g serving) due to soy sauce in dressing.
